title: Forty thousand is a lot of money
summary: An auction-house specialist gives a man two options for selling a painting that has been in his family for four generations. He chooses the one that pays less.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: Mr Werner. Please sit. I have the assessment.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: Yes.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: The painting is genuine. Eighteen-ninety-one, as we thought. The signature is consistent with the others we have on file from that period.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: That is good.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: The estimate, at auction, is between sixty-eight thousand and ninety thousand euros. Possibly more if there are competing collectors that evening.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: I see.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: Now. May I ask you something before we set the date?

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: Yes.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: Why are you selling it?

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: My grandmother left it to my mother. My mother left it to me. I do not have children. My nephews are interested but they are not interested for the right reason. They are interested in the number you just said.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: That is honest.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: I have read about your firm. You have a programme — a museum-acquisition route.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: We do.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: Tell me about it.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: For a percentage lower than open auction, we can place the work directly with a museum we know is interested. The painting becomes part of a public collection. It is studied. It is shown — not always, but periodically. The price you receive is about sixty per cent of the open auction estimate.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: So I would receive roughly forty thousand euros instead of around eighty thousand.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: Roughly. Plus or minus.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: Forty thousand is a lot of money.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: Forty thousand is a lot of money. Eighty thousand is more.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: Are you allowed to recommend one route over the other?

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: No.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: … will you take it to the museum anyway?

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: Yes. If that is what you choose.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: It is. Tell them my grandmother's name was Frieda.

body.speaker: Renaud
body.text: They will note the name on the label.

body.speaker: Werner
body.text: Then it is done.

annotations.meaningVi: việc thu nhận / mua vào (cho bảo tàng)
tấm biển ghi chú (bên cạnh hiện vật trong bảo tàng)

grammarNotes.title: Second conditional 'would' for hypothetical financial scenarios
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng 'would + V' để bàn về kịch bản giả định, đặc biệt với tiền bạc và lựa chọn. Trong bài: 'So I would receive roughly forty thousand euros instead of around eighty thousand.' (giả định nhận tiền theo phương án bảo tàng) 'will you take it to the museum anyway?' / 'Yes. If that is what you choose.' (kết hợp 'will' với điều kiện). Câu 'I would receive' so sánh hai kết quả của hai lựa chọn — phù hợp khi cân nhắc.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 'would + verb' to discuss hypothetical scenarios, especially around money and choice. The dialogue: 'So I would receive roughly forty thousand euros instead of around eighty thousand.' (hypothetical museum-route outcome) 'will you take it to the museum anyway?' / 'Yes. If that is what you choose.' (will + condition). 'I would receive' compares two outcomes of two choices — ideal for weighing options.

translationVi:
Renaud: Ông Werner. Mời ngồi. Tôi có kết quả thẩm định.
Werner: Vâng.
Renaud: Bức tranh là thật. Năm 1891, như ta đoán. Chữ ký khớp với những bức khác chúng tôi có trong hồ sơ thuộc giai đoạn đó.
Werner: Vậy là tốt.
Renaud: Định giá, tại đấu giá, là từ sáu mươi tám nghìn đến chín mươi nghìn euro. Có thể hơn nếu tối hôm đó có người sưu tập cạnh tranh.
Werner: Tôi hiểu.
Renaud: Bây giờ. Cho phép tôi hỏi ông một câu trước khi đặt ngày đấu giá?
Werner: Vâng.
Renaud: Vì sao ông bán?
Werner: Bà tôi để lại cho mẹ tôi. Mẹ tôi để lại cho tôi. Tôi không có con. Mấy đứa cháu trai tôi quan tâm nhưng không quan tâm vì lý do đúng. Chúng quan tâm con số bà vừa nói.
Renaud: Đó là câu trả lời trung thực.
Werner: Tôi đã đọc về công ty của bà. Có một chương trình — tuyến mua-vào bảo tàng.
Renaud: Có.
Werner: Bà kể tôi nghe đi.
Renaud: Với một tỉ lệ thấp hơn đấu giá mở, chúng tôi có thể đưa thẳng tác phẩm đến một bảo tàng mà chúng tôi biết quan tâm. Bức tranh trở thành một phần của bộ sưu tập công. Nó được nghiên cứu. Nó được trưng bày — không phải lúc nào, nhưng theo định kỳ. Giá ông nhận khoảng sáu mươi phần trăm so với định giá đấu giá mở.
Werner: Vậy tôi sẽ nhận khoảng bốn mươi nghìn euro thay vì khoảng tám mươi.
Renaud: Đại khái. Trên dưới.
Werner: Bốn mươi nghìn là nhiều tiền.
Renaud: Bốn mươi nghìn là nhiều tiền. Tám mươi nghìn nhiều hơn.
Werner: Bà có được phép gợi ý một tuyến hơn tuyến kia không?
Renaud: Không.
Werner: … bà có đem nó đến bảo tàng nếu tôi chọn vậy không?
Renaud: Có. Nếu đó là lựa chọn của ông.
Werner: Là vậy. Báo họ tên bà tôi là Frieda.
Renaud: Họ sẽ ghi tên trên nhãn.
Werner: Vậy là xong.

questions.prompt: What is the auction estimate range?
options: Between sixty-eight thousand and ninety thousand euros
questions.explanation: 'Between sixty-eight thousand and ninety thousand euros.'

options: No children; nephews are interested but only in the money.
Placing the work directly with a museum known to be interested; the painting joins a public collection.

questions.prompt: What price does Werner get on the museum route?
options: About sixty per cent of the open auction estimate (roughly forty thousand euros)

questions.prompt: How does Renaud respond to 'Forty thousand is a lot of money'?
options: She agrees, then adds 'Eighty thousand is more.'

questions.prompt: Is Renaud allowed to recommend one route?
What does he ask the museum to note on the label?
questions.explanation: 'Tell them my grandmother's name was Frieda.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A real choice between money and meaning can be a deliberate one — taking less to ensure a different kind of return for someone who is not there anymore.
questions.explanation: Werner trades half the money for the public label.
questions.hint: Notice what Werner buys with the lost money.

questions.prompt: Which is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That Renaud advised Werner to take the museum route.
That the museum receives the work for a lower percentage.
That the painting will be studied and shown.
questions.explanation: Renaud explicitly says 'No' when asked if she can recommend.

questions.prompt: What is Renaud's professional discipline in this dialogue?
options: She lays out both options accurately and refuses to recommend.
She pushes Werner to choose auction.
She pushes him to choose museum.
questions.hint: Notice the 'No' to 'are you allowed to recommend'.

Renaud: Mr Werner. Please sit. I have the {{b1}}.
Werner: Yes.
Renaud: The painting is genuine. Eighteen-ninety-one, as we thought. The signature is consistent with the others we have on file from that period.
Werner: That is good.

Werner: Yes.
Renaud: Why are you selling it?
Werner: My {{b3}} left it to my mother. My mother left it to me. I do not have children. My nephews are interested but they are not interested for the right reason. They are interested in the number you just said.
Renaud: That is honest.
Werner: I have read about your firm. You have a programme — a museum-{{b4}} route.
Renaud: We do.
Werner: Tell me about it.
Renaud: For a percentage lower than open auction, we can place the work directly with a {{b5}} we know is interested. The painting becomes part of a public collection. It is studied. It is shown — not always, but periodically. The price you receive is about {{b6}} per cent of the open auction estimate.
Werner: So I would receive roughly forty thousand euros instead of around {{b7}} thousand.
Renaud: Roughly. Plus or minus.
Werner: Forty thousand is a lot of money.
Renaud: Forty thousand is a lot of money. Eighty thousand is {{b8}}.
Werner: Are you allowed to {{b9}} one route over the other?
Renaud: No.
Werner: … will you take it to the museum anyway?
Renaud: Yes. If that is what you choose.
Werner: It is. Tell them my grandmother's name was {{b10}}.
Renaud: They will note the name on the label.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Werner trades forty thousand euros for his grandmother's name on a label. Is there a choice you have made — or are about to make — where the more expensive option is to take less money in exchange for a different kind of return? What is your version of 'the name on the label'?
